fingerprint: correctness:when an inconclusive condition co-occurs with a restorable ref/index mutation in the same run, the combined-precedence criterion ('all detected mutations that are classifiable as restorable are still restored before the dominant reason is recorded') contradicts the inconclusive/stash safety rule ('do not auto-restore in v1' / 'take no action that would make the dangling work harder to recover'). the contract does not specify whether restoration is suppressed when inconclusive is the dominant reason.
message: When an inconclusive condition co-occurs with a restorable ref/index mutation in the same run, the combined-precedence criterion ('all detected mutations that are classifiable as restorable are still restored before the dominant reason is recorded') contradicts the inconclusive/stash safety rule ('do NOT auto-restore in v1' / 'take no action that would make the dangling work harder to recover'). The contract does not specify whether restoration is suppressed when inconclusive is the dominant reason.
material_impact: Example: agent creates branch refs/heads/wip then `git reset --hard` to the original HEAD. Detection = inconclusive (HEAD unchanged, reflog advanced) AND a restorable ref mutation (wip). Restoring the ref means deleting wip, which removes the only anchor for the reset-hard'd commit and pushes that work to dangling/fsck-only — directly violating the 'never silently lose work / don't make recovery harder' invariant. An implementer cannot tell whether to restore the ref or leave it, so the safety-critical path is ambiguous and could be implemented to destroy recoverability.
fix_direction: Add an explicit rule: when the dominant terminal_reason is executor_git_guard_inconclusive, suppress all auto-restoration (do not delete/move/reset any ref, index, or HEAD) so anchors for dangling work are preserved; restore restorable mutations only when the dominant reason is itself a restorable class (history/ref/index) or restore_failed.
uncertainty: The author may intend 'classifiable as restorable' to exclude mutations entangled with an inconclusive condition, but this is not stated; the conflict is real as written.

fingerprint: validation:the test enumeration omits a standalone executor_git_restore_failed scenario even though the criterion 'if any guard restore command fails ... records executor_git_restore_failed' is specified.
message: The test enumeration omits a standalone executor_git_restore_failed scenario even though the criterion 'If any guard restore command fails ... records executor_git_restore_failed' is specified.
material_impact: The restore_failed classification path can ship unverified; a regression there would not be caught by the contract's own validation set.
fix_direction: Add a TestGitGuard… scenario that forces a guard restore command to fail (e.g., make a ref unwritable / inject a failing write-git path) and asserts terminal_reason == executor_git_restore_failed with diagnostic restore-error detail recorded.

fingerprint: quality:the 'restore would conflict -> inconclusive' trigger is not distinguished from executor_git_restore_failed and may be unreachable in v1.
message: The 'restore would conflict -> inconclusive' trigger is not distinguished from executor_git_restore_failed and may be unreachable in v1.
material_impact: An implementer cannot tell what concretely constitutes a pre-emptive 'restore would conflict' inconclusive case versus attempting the restore and recording executor_git_restore_failed, leaving the detection logic under-specified or producing dead code.
fix_direction: Either define the concrete conflict condition that should pre-empt to inconclusive before attempting restore, or remove the trigger and let restore failures fall through to executor_git_restore_failed in v1.

fingerprint: quality:the executor_git_ref_mutation 'unless the case is ambiguous' qualifier has no defined ambiguity threshold or fallback terminal_reason.
message: The executor_git_ref_mutation 'unless the case is ambiguous' qualifier has no defined ambiguity threshold or fallback terminal_reason.
material_impact: An implementer cannot determine when a ref change (moved branch, deleted ref, ref pointing at dangling work) is 'safely restorable' vs 'ambiguous', nor which terminal_reason to record when ambiguous (presumably executor_git_guard_inconclusive), producing inconsistent classification.
fix_direction: Enumerate what makes a ref mutation ambiguous (e.g., a ref now points at an object that would become dangling on restore, or a ref was deleted whose target is not in the snapshot) and state the fallback terminal_reason explicitly.

fingerprint: quality:the relationship between the new git-guard terminal_reason field and any pre-existing attempt/process terminal_reason is only loosely specified in design/gate prose.
message: The relationship between the new git-guard terminal_reason field and any pre-existing attempt/process terminal_reason is only loosely specified in DESIGN/gate prose.
material_impact: If implemented as the shared attempt terminal_reason rather than a dedicated git-guard field, normal transport-failure terminal_reasons could block the gate (false positives) or overwrite the git-guard reason, defeating the loud-block design.
fix_direction: State explicitly that the git-guard outcome is a dedicated field and that next/gate suppression keys off that field specifically, independent of any transport/loop terminal_reason.

fingerprint: validation:the four validation commands do not enforce the enumerated per-scenario test coverage. `grep -rq 'func testgitguard'` and `go test -run 'testgitguard'` pass with any subset of testgitguard* tests, so individual scenario criteria (e.g. commit-then-reset-hard classified as inconclusive, review-fix staging preservation, precedence inconclusive>restore_failed) have no command that fails if their test is omitted.
message: The four validation commands do not enforce the enumerated per-scenario test coverage. `grep -rq 'func TestGitGuard'` and `go test -run 'TestGitGuard'` pass with any subset of TestGitGuard* tests, so individual scenario criteria (e.g. commit-then-reset-hard classified as inconclusive, review-fix staging preservation, precedence inconclusive>restore_failed) have no command that fails if their test is omitted.
material_impact: A partial implementation covering only a couple of TestGitGuard scenarios would pass all four validation commands, so the prose scenario list is not mechanically guaranteed to be exercised.
fix_direction: Either name the required test functions exactly and add a grep per scenario, or add a coverage/assert step that fails when an expected TestGitGuard* function is missing.
uncertainty: Partly inherent to Go's -run matching; the TestGitGuard prefix + explicit scenario list already mitigate it, which is why this is advisory.

fingerprint: validation:a few acceptance criteria embed prose-only, subjective qualifiers with no machine-checkable outcome ('enough restore error detail for a human to diagnose', 'preserves existing successful execute/review-fix behavior').
message: A few acceptance criteria embed prose-only, subjective qualifiers with no machine-checkable outcome ('enough restore error detail for a human to diagnose', 'preserves existing successful execute/review-fix behavior').
material_impact: These qualitative phrases cannot be directly asserted; a test can only verify the companion checkable facts (error field non-empty; existing suite/make check passes), leaving the 'enough'/'preserves behavior' judgment unverified.
fix_direction: Reduce each to a checkable form: e.g. 'restore error outcome field is non-empty and contains the failing command and git stderr', and rely on the existing test suite under make check for behavior preservation.
uncertainty: Companion clauses already make the field-presence portions checkable, so the residual gap is small.

fingerprint: validation:validation commands verify existence of at least one testgitguard function and that all present testgitguard tests pass, but do not enforce that the specific high-value scenarios enumerated in the acceptance criteria are actually implemented. a thin implementation with a single happy-path testgitguard test would satisfy `grep -rq 'func testgitguard'` and `go test -run 'testgitguard'` while omitting commit-then-reset-hard classification, terminal_reason precedence, review-fix staging preservation, agent-error-after-mutation, etc.
message: Validation commands verify existence of at least one TestGitGuard function and that all present TestGitGuard tests pass, but do not enforce that the specific high-value scenarios enumerated in the acceptance criteria are actually implemented. A thin implementation with a single happy-path TestGitGuard test would satisfy `grep -rq 'func TestGitGuard'` and `go test -run 'TestGitGuard'` while omitting commit-then-reset-hard classification, terminal_reason precedence, review-fix staging preservation, agent-error-after-mutation, etc.
material_impact: Not a soundness failure — the commands fail correctly on a missing or broken implementation; this only weakens recall of the scenario coverage, which is a completeness preference, hence advisory.
fix_direction: Optionally strengthen validation by greping for the specifically named scenario functions (e.g. add greps for `TestGitGuardInconclusiveResetHard`, `TestGitGuardReviewFixStagingPreservation`, `TestGitGuardAgentError`) so the gate verifies the critical-path tests exist, not just any TestGitGuard test.
uncertainty: This is an inherent limitation of name-prefix-based test validation rather than a flaw introduced by this contract; many sound Go contracts accept it.

fingerprint: scope:scope.out forbids mutating repository history during execute/review-fix handling, but scope.in and the design require the guard itself to run `git reset --mixed` and restore refs/reflogs, which are history/ref mutations. the 'as part of normal ... agent handling' qualifier resolves this, but the contradiction is stated only implicitly.
message: scope.out forbids mutating repository history during execute/review-fix handling, but scope.in and the design require the guard itself to run `git reset --mixed` and restore refs/reflogs, which are history/ref mutations. The 'as part of normal ... agent handling' qualifier resolves this, but the contradiction is stated only implicitly.
material_impact: A literal reader could interpret the scope.out exclusion as prohibiting the guard's own restoration writes, since reset --mixed and ref/reflog restoration do mutate repository state.
fix_direction: Make the carve-out explicit in scope.out, e.g. append '(this exclusion does not apply to the guard's own controlled restoration writes — reset --mixed and ref/reflog restore — which are in scope per the guard design)'.
uncertainty: The existing 'as part of normal ... agent handling' qualifier likely already disambiguates this for most implementers; raising it for precision, not because the implementation would be blocked.

fingerprint: correctness:the execute clean-tree precondition assumes the guarded working tree is free of pactum's own run artifacts, but pactum writes a tracked ledger file and an untracked run directory into that tree, which would make porcelain non-empty and bail every execute attempt as inconclusive before the agent runs.
message: The execute clean-tree precondition assumes the guarded working tree is free of pactum's own run artifacts, but pactum writes a tracked ledger file and an untracked run directory into that tree, which would make porcelain non-empty and bail every execute attempt as inconclusive before the agent runs.
material_impact: If pactum's run-record/ledger writes land in the guarded tree (as they do here), the clean-tree precondition fails on pactum's own bookkeeping, so the guard finishes `executor_git_guard_inconclusive` and never runs the execute agent — the guard is inert in the dogfood environment and any deployment where artifacts share the tree.
fix_direction: Surface the assumption explicitly and resolve it: state that pactum's run artifacts must live outside the guarded working tree, or specify that the clean-tree porcelain check excludes pactum's own artifact paths; add an acceptance criterion covering it.
uncertainty: Could not run the confirming gitignore/check-ignore command (permission declined). It is possible `.heurema/` is excluded by config I have not seen, or that the execute-time guarded repo is a checkout distinct from pactum's own tree.

fingerprint: correctness:detection and recovery lean on git reflog being enabled, but reflog logging (core.logallrefupdates) can be disabled; this assumption is not surfaced as a precondition or assumption.
message: Detection and recovery lean on git reflog being enabled, but reflog logging (core.logAllRefUpdates) can be disabled; this assumption is not surfaced as a precondition or assumption.
material_impact: In a reflog-disabled repo the reflog detector never fires and the 'accessible via git reflog' guarantee is false; detection still survives via the commit-object-set snapshot, but that makes the object-set authoritative and the reflog supplementary — the inverse of how step 3 reads, risking an implementer relying on the reflog path.
fix_direction: Add an explicit assumption/precondition that reflog logging is enabled, or restate that the commit-object-set is the authoritative detector and the reflog is supplementary, and soften the 'accessible via git reflog' recovery claim.

fingerprint: correctness:the snapshot-before / compare-after model implicitly assumes agenttransport().run returning means all of the agent's git side-effects have landed; background/detached git processes that outlive the acp turn are not addressed.
message: The snapshot-before / compare-after model implicitly assumes agentTransport().Run returning means all of the agent's git side-effects have landed; background/detached git processes that outlive the ACP turn are not addressed.
material_impact: A git write that completes after Run returns could land after the compare (missed → silent history mutation, proceed normally) or race the restore — exactly the indirection class the design set out to defeat.
fix_direction: Surface the assumption that Run fully quiesces the agent's git activity (no surviving background processes), or document post-Run async writes as a known v1 limitation.

fingerprint: correctness:the index baseline and index restore are defined solely via the index tree hash, implicitly assuming index state is fully represented by its tree.
message: The index baseline and index restore are defined solely via the index tree hash, implicitly assuming index state is fully represented by its tree.
material_impact: A tree hash omits stat metadata and cannot represent intent-to-add entries (write-tree errors on them); index changes producing the same tree are invisible to detection, and tree-based restore can leave files spuriously dirty — divergence between the spec's promise and observed behavior.
fix_direction: Make explicit that the index is treated as content-only (tree-equivalent) and that intent-to-add/stat fidelity are out of v1 scope, or capture index state more completely if fidelity matters.

fingerprint: quality:terminal_reason precedence is specified only among the five git-guard reasons; the assumption that no pre-existing (transport/retry) lifecycle terminal_reason competes for the same field is implicit.
message: terminal_reason precedence is specified only among the five git-guard reasons; the assumption that no pre-existing (transport/retry) lifecycle terminal_reason competes for the same field is implicit.
material_impact: If an existing lifecycle path also sets terminal_reason, precedence between git-guard and non-git-guard reasons is undefined, leaving the implementer to guess whether the guard overwrites or coexists — affecting next/gate suppression.
fix_direction: State explicitly that the git-guard reason is authoritative for the field at the integration point (or define precedence vs pre-existing terminal_reasons).

fingerprint: correctness:the contract requires restoring changed refs' reflogs, but the snapshot only requires storing reflog line counts or hashes, which is insufficient data to restore the original reflog contents.
message: The contract requires restoring changed refs' reflogs, but the snapshot only requires storing reflog line counts or hashes, which is insufficient data to restore the original reflog contents.
material_impact: An implementer cannot both follow the specified snapshot shape and satisfy the restore requirement. A line count or hash can detect reflog advancement, but it cannot reconstruct prior reflog entries after guard-owned ref writes add new reflog records. This could lead to an implementation that restores refs but leaves reflogs mutated, violating the stated goal of preserving git history/refs/reflog/index intact.
fix_direction: Either require the snapshot to capture full reflog contents plus an explicit restoration strategy, or narrow v1 acceptance to restoring refs/index only while detecting and reporting reflog mutation without attempting reflog restoration.

fingerprint: validation:the restore-failure diagnostic acceptance criterion is not fully machine-checkable because it requires 'enough restore error detail' without defining the required observable fields.
message: The restore-failure diagnostic acceptance criterion is not fully machine-checkable because it requires 'enough restore error detail' without defining the required observable fields.
material_impact: Tests can verify that some restore error field exists, but cannot objectively determine whether the detail is 'enough'; implementers and reviewers may disagree while all validation commands still pass.
fix_direction: Specify the exact machine-checkable restore error fields, for example failed operation, git command name/args, exit code, stderr/stdout excerpt, affected ref or target hash, and whether the dominant terminal reason was overridden by an inconclusive condition.

fingerprint: validation:the targeted testgitguard validation can pass vacuously if the required scenario tests are absent or skipped, because go test -run succeeds when no tests match and the grep command only proves that some testgitguard function exists.
message: The targeted TestGitGuard validation can pass vacuously if the required scenario tests are absent or skipped, because go test -run succeeds when no tests match and the grep command only proves that some TestGitGuard function exists.
material_impact: A weak implementation could include a single trivial `TestGitGuard...` function while omitting several required scenarios, and these two commands would not reliably catch that by themselves.
fix_direction: Add explicit non-vacuity checks for the required named tests, or use a small script/Go test that enumerates expected TestGitGuard test names and fails if any are missing.

fingerprint: scope:the scope is under-broad for the reset-hard/no-work-loss guarantee because it does not include any working-tree content backup or recovery mechanism.
message: The scope is under-broad for the reset-hard/no-work-loss guarantee because it does not include any working-tree content backup or recovery mechanism.
material_impact: An implementation following this scope cannot guarantee preservation of uncommitted working-tree edits that are deleted by `git reset --hard`; there may be no commit, stash, or dangling object to recover from. The implementation would either fail the acceptance criteria or falsely claim safety for an unrecoverable case.
fix_direction: Either narrow the acceptance language to commit/stash-backed reset-hard cases only, or add an in-scope pre-agent working-tree content backup/journal and recovery path for destructive working-tree resets.

fingerprint: scope:the stash handling scope is ambiguous because stash refs are listed among safely restorable non-current refs while stash creation is also explicitly out of auto-restore scope.
message: The stash handling scope is ambiguous because stash refs are listed among safely restorable non-current refs while stash creation is also explicitly out of auto-restore scope.
material_impact: A reader could route `refs/stash` through the generic ref-restore path and accidentally drop or rewrite stash recovery data, contrary to the intended v1 inconclusive behavior.
fix_direction: Remove `stash refs` from the safely-restorable ref examples, or explicitly state that all `refs/stash` mutations are classified as `executor_git_guard_inconclusive` in v1.
uncertainty: The later stash-specific acceptance criterion likely expresses the intended behavior, so this is mainly a scope clarity risk rather than a hard contradiction.

fingerprint: quality:the contract does not explicitly state the concurrency assumption that no other process mutates git state during a guarded agent attempt.
message: The contract does not explicitly state the concurrency assumption that no other process mutates git state during a guarded agent attempt.
material_impact: A concurrent user/tool/background process changing refs, reflogs, objects, or the index could be classified as an agent mutation, causing inconclusive termination or restoration behavior that surprises operators.
fix_direction: Add an explicit assumption that the guarded repository is not concurrently mutated during execute or review-fix attempts, or define how such concurrent mutations should be classified.
